text: The surgeon uses ion navigation bronchoscopy to preoperatively mark the tumor in the medial basilar segment. Ports are placed for the robotic-assisted segmentectomy, and the Firefly imaging system is used to localize the tumor marked with ICG dye and methylene blue. The tumor is visible in the medial basilar segment, and cautery is used to further mark its location.

The surgeon begins the procedure with a mediastinal lymph node dissection, elevating the basilar segments of the right lower lobe to access and remove the station nine and eight lymph nodes. The inferior pulmonary ligament is taken down, and the inferior pulmonary vein is isolated for further dissection. The surgeon uses bipolar cautery at the highest setting to manage bleeding during the dissection. The posterior mediastinal pleura is dissected, and the subcarinal space is accessed to harvest station seven lymph nodes, with assistance in manipulating the lung segments.

The surgeon performs a complete lymphadenectomy, using a vessel sealer extend to dissect subcarinal and paratracheal lymph nodes, aiming for a drier surgical field. The posterior segment of the right upper lobe is pushed up to expose the backside of the right main stem bronchus. Dissection focuses on the space between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, revealing the station XI sump lymph node, which is carefully dissected despite some bleeding.

The surgeon exposes the major fissure from the backside, revealing a large lymph node within the fissure. Using bipolar cautery, the surgeon carefully dissects the lymph node, aiming to expose the interlobar pulmonary artery beneath it. The inferior pulmonary vein is dissected to identify the segmental branches, focusing on segment seven, which is located between the highest and lowest branches.

The surgeon uses gloved fingertips to harvest lymph nodes, revealing the pulmonary artery. They access the space above the inferior pulmonary vein to divide the medial aspect of the major fissure. Using a vessel for traction, the surgeon dilates the space and inserts an endolead catheter to safely pass a stapler through the avascular plane, dividing the lung parenchyma and exposing the interlobar pulmonary artery from the medial aspect.

The surgeon exposes the medial segmental artery (A7), the most medial branch of the interlobar pulmonary artery, and dissects the basilar trunk. A small portion of lung parenchyma remains between the artery and the divided fissure, which is carefully divided using a stapler to minimize air leaks.

The surgeon uses a curved bipolar dissector to precisely dissect segmental vessels, focusing on the A-seven segmental pulmonary artery branch. An endolyte catheter is positioned across the artery, followed by the use of a SureForm-45 stapler with a gray load to divide the artery. Below the artery, the segmental bronchus is exposed, with minimal lymph node encasement. The surgeon uses a blue load to divide the bronchus, resulting in parenchymal bleeding, which is controlled using sponge rolls and suction. The segmental veins draining S-seven are then dissected.

The surgeon uses a curved bipolar dissector to focus on the segmental veins draining segment seven, viewed from the medial side. The inferior pulmonary vein and its branches are visible, with a larger venous branch at the bottom and a smaller one at the top. The pulmonary artery is located below these veins. A gray load stapler is used to divide the venous drainage to segment seven, preparing for the division of the parenchyma in the intersegmental plane. The endolyd catheter aids in guiding the stapler through the tricky angle.

The surgeon begins to transect the lung parenchyma using a blue load stapler, ensuring a margin is maintained from the tumor marked with methylene blue and ICG. The lung is manipulated to stay below the cut edge of the segmental bronchus, with careful attention to the geometry of the basilar segments. The surgeon flips the lung to continue transection from the backside, aiming to connect the planes while maintaining a safe margin from the tumor.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler from the backside to adjust the geometry of the lung parenchyma resection. As the procedure progresses towards the center of the lobe, thicker staple fires are required, utilizing green and black loads. The surgeon ensures the staple line remains below the cut edge of the segmental bronchus. The resected specimen is placed in a ripstop nylon bag for inspection and sent for frozen section analysis to assess the margin. Additional lung parenchyma is resected to increase the margin, using green loads from the medial staple port.

The surgeon uses the posterior staple port to fire additional staples, ensuring pneumostatic interparenchymal staple lines with minimal air leaks. The S-seven medial basilar segmentectomy is visible, along with the removal of extra segmental lymph nodes. The lung is reflected inferiorly to dissect out the station 2R and 4R lymph nodes as a nodal packet.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer extend to dissect paratracheal and subcarinal nodes, packing the area with Fibrillar for hemostasis. A fibrin sealant patch, Tacoseal, is applied to the staple line to prevent air leaks. An intercostal nerve block is performed with bupivacaine. The chest tube is placed to the apex, and the lung is inflated, showing the completed S-seven segmentectomy.
